Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd tell myself I was going to study for like 30 hours a week, and sometimes I actually did, but it was never really organized. I'd just kind of drift through the material when I felt like it, usually late at night when my anxiety was keeping me awake anyway. I'm a visual learner so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and color-coded notes, but the actual scheduling part? That was a mess.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, and then I'd have these intense catch-up sessions right before deadlines. The sadness I've been dealing with made it really hard to stick to any kind of regular schedule. When I was feeling particularly low, even opening my laptop felt like climbing a mountain.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about something due and that would jolt me into action. Occasionally I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, but honestly, I didn't make it a habit. The LMS always felt kind of cold and overwhelming to navigate when I was already feeling anxious.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got into a comfortable rhythm with the LMS. There were maybe a couple weeks where I'd check in more regularly, but then something would happen—I'd have a bad mental health day or get overwhelmed with my extracurriculars—and the routine would fall apart. It never became automatic for me the way I think it did for other students.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly the most helpful because I could watch them when I couldn't sleep and actually absorb information visually. I watched a lot of them, like hours and hours worth, because seeing concepts explained helped more than reading about them. Any visual resources like diagrams or flowcharts really clicked for me. The discussion boards weren't my thing though—I only posted twice the whole course.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums entirely. The idea of posting my thoughts publicly when I was already feeling so sad and anxious was just too much. I also delayed a lot of the readings because walls of text don't work well for how I learn. If something wasn't visual or didn't have a direct deadline attached, it usually got pushed to the bottom of my list indefinitely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to an assignment grade. Visual content that I could actually understand without reading paragraphs of text felt worthwhile. Honestly, when you're dealing with high anxiety and limited resources, you have to be really selective about where you put your energy. If something felt like busywork, I just couldn't justify spending my limited mental bandwidth on it.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. When you're barely keeping your head above water emotionally, optional means 'not happening.' Required activities got done because they had to, but even then I'd often leave them until the last possible moment. There was this constant calculation in my head about what I absolutely had to do versus what I could let slide.
Q9: Oh absolutely, I did so much studying offline that never showed up anywhere. I'd download videos and PDFs and then work from them on my tablet when I didn't have good internet or when I just couldn't face logging into the system. I made tons of handwritten visual notes and diagrams. I also used YouTube videos and free resources online to help explain concepts because sometimes the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd be studying from printed materials or my own notes, completely disconnected from the LMS. I'd be actively working through problems, making mind maps, rewatching downloaded lectures—but none of that would show up as activity. Sometimes my best learning happened when I was sitting in a café with my notebook because being at home with my laptop made my anxiety worse.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a lecture or reading once and then use it for days or weeks afterward. With my limited resources, I couldn't always count on having reliable internet, so downloading and working offline just made more sense. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I might have reviewed it ten times from my downloads.
Q12: The hardest times were when my anxiety spiked and the sadness got really heavy. There were weeks where getting out of bed felt impossible, let alone engaging with coursework. When I had too many things competing for my attention—extracurriculars, trying to manage my mental health, dealing with financial stress from having low resources—the course was the first thing to slip. I'd still complete assignments somehow, but meaningful engagement disappeared.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or confused, I'd just click through things without really absorbing them. The workload felt crushing sometimes, especially when my mental state was already fragile. I'd log in, stare at the screen, feel overwhelmed, and log out having accomplished nothing. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS often got the bare minimum of my attention.
Q14: So many times. I'd have a video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, consumed by worry or sadness. I'd scroll through readings without any of it registering. There were sessions where I was technically 'studying' but I couldn't tell you a single thing I'd learned afterward. My body was present but my mind had checked out.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because the social anxiety aspect was too much. I'd avoid starting assignments until the last minute because the thought of beginning felt paralyzing. Even when I knew something was important, the anxiety would make me freeze up. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. My whole engagement pattern revolved around due dates—I'd ignore something for weeks and then scramble in the final hours. The LMS became a panic station rather than a learning tool. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all during my worst periods.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag for me. Good feedback gave me a temporary boost, but negative feedback would send me into an anxiety spiral that made me avoid the course for days. I'd sometimes delay looking at grades because I was scared of what I'd see. When feedback was constructive and specific, it helped me focus my limited energy on what actually mattered.
Q18: The structure having 14 online courses meant everything felt fragmented and overwhelming. I couldn't keep track of what was due where, which fed into my anxiety. The course structure didn't really accommodate someone who was struggling mentally—it assumed consistent engagement that I just couldn't provide. Having everything online made it easier to drift away and harder to feel connected.
Q19: The visual resources and video content made engagement easier for me as a visual learner. But the discussion requirements were really hard—I only managed two posts all semester because of my anxiety. The sheer volume of content across all my courses made it harder to engage deeply with anything. Clearer visual roadmaps of what to do when would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my bad days—clicking around, watching videos, trying to engage but being too overwhelmed to actually produce anything. High activity might mean someone is struggling to understand and keeps revisiting material. Or they might be anxious and checking things repeatedly without making progress. Activity doesn't equal productivity, especially when you're dealing with mental health stuff.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I completed almost 95% of assignments and got decent grades, but my LMS engagement was low. A lot of my real learning happened offline with downloaded materials and my own notes. Someone can understand the content and perform well without constantly being logged in. The LMS only captures one slice of how learning actually happens.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, but they miss everything about the quality of that engagement. They can't see that I was crying while watching a lecture or that I understood more from my hand-drawn diagram than from any online activity. They miss all the offline work, the mental health struggles, the real reasons behind the numbers.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding what you're learning, not just going through the motions. For me, real engagement happened in quiet moments with my notebook, making visual connections between ideas. It's about your mind being present and curious, not just your browser being open. You can be deeply engaged without anyone being able to measure it.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought against my anxiety and sadness just to complete assignments. It can't capture the mindfulness exercises I did to calm myself enough to study, or how much the intervention actually helped me cope. It doesn't show the hours of offline visual note-making or the YouTube tutorials I used. Most importantly, it can't show that despite feeling like a drifter, I actually cared—I was just struggling in ways that don't leave digital footprints.
